tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • INC: 1 occurrences<>
  • LEA: 2 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. The GNU Fortran compiler does not support allocation alignment and does not feature directives to benefit from aligned data.
  2. Use another compiler or locally use C code (for instance via libraries)
<>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 1 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.
hint.title: Conversion instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
8 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
8 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).
5 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).
14 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 140 FP arithmetical operations:
  • 77: addition or subtraction (63 inside FMA instructions)
  • 63: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 577 bytes (72 double precision FP elements). The binary loop is storing 4 bytes (0 double precision FP elements).

hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.24 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 28.33 cycles. At this rate:
  • 21% of peak load performance is reached (20.36 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 0% of peak store performance is reached (0.14 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.33 to 18.50 cycles (1.53x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
The number of fused uops of the instruction [CLTQ] is unknown
20% of peak computational performance is used (4.94 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.33 to 18.50 cycles (1.53x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly: Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)<>
    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
56% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 57% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 72% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 53% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 41% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.33 to 20.44 cycles (1.39x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • INC: 1 occurrences<>
  • LEA: 2 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. The GNU Fortran compiler does not support allocation alignment and does not feature directives to benefit from aligned data.
  2. Use another compiler or locally use C code (for instance via libraries)
<>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 1 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
8 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).

14 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 140 FP arithmetical operations:
  • 77: addition or subtraction (63 inside FMA instructions)
  • 63: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 577 bytes (72 double precision FP elements). The binary loop is storing 4 bytes (0 double precision FP elements).

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 28.33 cycles. At this rate:
  • 21% of peak load performance is reached (20.36 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 0% of peak store performance is reached (0.14 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.33 to 18.50 cycles (1.53x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
The number of fused uops of the instruction [CLTQ] is unknown
20% of peak computational performance is used (4.94 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.33 to 18.50 cycles (1.53x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly: Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)<>
    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
56% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 57% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 72% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 53% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 41% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.33 to 20.44 cycles (1.39x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

header:
The loop is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-OMP/MG/mg.f90:953-956. Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 254 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=254
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option. This loop has 254 execution paths. The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck. Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX
Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)
